edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah's paramilitary wing is the Jihad Council
edges.context: Jihad Council is the paramilitary wing of Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: its political wing is the Loyalty to the Resistance Bloc party in the Lebanese Parliament
edges.context: Loyalty to the Resistance Bloc is the political wing of Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: The group was initially supported by 1,500 Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) instructors, who helped unify various Lebanese Shia factions under Hezbollah's leadership.
edges.context: IRGC trained and organized Hezbollah in its early years.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, Hezbollah fought against Israeli forces and the South Lebanon Army (SLA)
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the SLA.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah emerged in South Lebanon during a consolidation of Shia militias as a rival to the older Amal Movement.
edges.context: Hezbollah was a rival to Amal Movement.

edges.evidence_quote: Whether the Islamic Jihad Organisation (IJO) was a nom de guerre used by Hezbollah or a separate organisation, is disputed.
edges.context: IJO may be a pseudonym for Hezbollah.

edges.target: Lebanese Brigades to Fight the Israeli Occupation
edges.evidence_quote: In 1997, Hezbollah formed the multi-confessional Lebanese Brigades to Fight the Israeli Occupation
edges.context: Hezbollah created this group.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah established strong ties with Iran and is part of the " Axis of Resistance ".
edges.context: Hezbollah is a member of the Axis of Resistance.

edges.evidence_quote: Paramilitary wing: Jihad Council , Lebanese Resistance Brigades
edges.context: Lebanese Resistance Brigades is listed as a paramilitary wing of Hezbollah in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: National affiliation: March 8 Alliance
edges.context: Hezbollah is part of the March 8 Alliance.

edges.evidence_quote: The group was initially supported by 1,500 Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) instructors, who helped unify various Lebanese Shia factions under Hezbollah's leadership.
edges.context: IRGC provided instructors and support to unify Shia factions under Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, Hezbollah fought against Israeli forces and the South Lebanon Army (SLA), eventually leading to Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon in 2000.
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the SLA during the 1980s and 1990s.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah along with Amal is one of two major political parties in Lebanon that represent Shiite Muslims.
edges.context: Hezbollah and Amal are both major Shia political parties in Lebanon.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2006, Michel Aoun and Hassan Nasrallah met in Mar Mikhayel Church, Chiyah, and signed a memorandum of understanding between Free Patriotic Movement and Hezbollah organising their relation and discussing Hezbollah's disarmament with some conditions.
edges.context: Hezbollah and Free Patriotic Movement signed a memorandum of understanding in 2006, forming an alliance.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah-led opposition fighters seized control of several West Beirut neighbourhoods from Future Movement militiamen loyal to the backed government, in street battles that left 11 dead and 30 wounded.
edges.context: Hezbollah fighters clashed with Future Movement militiamen in May 2008, resulting in casualties.

edges.evidence_quote: The Hizbullah and Amal Bloc controls together six portfolios in the government.
edges.context: Hezbollah and Amal Movement share a parliamentary bloc and jointly control government portfolios.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah operates a satellite television station, Al-Manar TV ("the Lighthouse"), and a radio station, al-Nour ("the Light").
edges.context: Hezbollah owns and operates Al-Manar TV as its media outlet.

edges.evidence_quote: The group was initially supported by 1,500 Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) instructors, who helped unify various Lebanese Shia factions under Hezbollah's leadership.
edges.context: IRGC provided instructors and support to Hezbollah at its founding.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah's counterintelligence was initially managed by Iran's Quds Force
edges.context: Quds Force initially managed Hezbollah's counterintelligence operations.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, Hezbollah fought against Israeli forces and the South Lebanon Army (SLA), eventually leading to Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon in 2000.
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the South Lebanon Army during the 1980s and 1990s.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah has been one of the main parties of the March 8 Alliance since March 2005.
edges.context: Hezbollah is a main party in the March 8 Alliance since 2005.

edges.evidence_quote: Although Hezbollah had joined the new government in 2005, it remained staunchly opposed to the March 14 Alliance.
edges.context: Hezbollah opposed the March 14 Alliance politically.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah's paramilitary wing is the Jihad Council, and its political wing is the Loyalty to the Resistance Bloc party in the Lebanese Parliament.
edges.context: Loyalty to the Resistance Bloc is the political wing of Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah's paramilitary wing is the Jihad Council
edges.context: Jihad Council is the paramilitary wing of Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah has a military branch known as the Jihad Council, one component of which is Al-Muqawama al-Islamiyya ("The Islamic Resistance")
edges.context: Al-Muqawama al-Islamiyya is a component of Hezbollah's military branch.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah ... is the possible sponsor of a number of lesser-known militant groups, some of which may be little more than fronts for Hezbollah itself, including the Organisation of the Oppressed
edges.context: Organisation of the Oppressed is possibly a front group sponsored by Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah ... is the possible sponsor of a number of lesser-known militant groups, some of which may be little more than fronts for Hezbollah itself, including ... the Revolutionary Justice Organisation
edges.context: Revolutionary Justice Organisation is possibly a front group sponsored by Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah ... is the possible sponsor of a number of lesser-known militant groups, some of which may be little more than fronts for Hezbollah itself, including ... the Organisation of Right Against Wrong
edges.context: Organisation of Right Against Wrong is possibly a front group sponsored by Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah ... is the possible sponsor of a number of lesser-known militant groups, some of which may be little more than fronts for Hezbollah itself, including ... Followers of the Prophet Muhammad.
edges.context: Followers of the Prophet Muhammad is possibly a front group sponsored by Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: The Lebanese Resistance Brigades ... were formed by Hezbollah in 1997 as a multi-faith volunteer force to combat the Israeli occupation of Southern Lebanon.
edges.context: Hezbollah formed the Lebanese Resistance Brigades in 1997.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah is also associated with Al-Ahed ("the Covenant"), a weekly newspaper and online platform that publishes news reports, editorials, and ideological material aligned with the organisation's positions.
edges.context: Hezbollah is associated with Al-Ahed, its affiliated media outlet.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1982, the Palestine Liberation Organisation (PLO) was based in Southern Lebanon and was firing Katyusha rockets into northern Israel from Lebanon. Israel invaded Lebanon to evict the PLO, and Hezbollah became an armed organisation to expel the Israelis.
edges.context: Hezbollah emerged as an armed organization to expel Israelis after the PLO was based in Lebanon.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah's strength was enhanced by the dispatching of one thousand to two thousand members of the Iranian Revolutionary Guards and the financial backing of Iran.
edges.context: IRGC members were dispatched to support Hezbollah, enhancing its strength.

edges.evidence_quote: With the collapse of the SLA, and the rapid advance of Hezbollah forces, Israel withdrew on 24 May 2000.
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the SLA, leading to its collapse.

edges.evidence_quote: Ansar Allah, a Palestinian group closely associated with Hezbollah, claimed responsibility.
edges.context: Ansar Allah is closely associated with Hezbollah and claimed responsibility for attacks on behalf of Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: The attacks came after Hezbollah expressed support and praise for the Hamas attacks on Israel.
edges.context: Hezbollah expressed support and praise for Hamas attacks on Israel.

edges.evidence_quote: Since it was established, Hezbollah has been heavily dependent on Iran's IRGC for funds and equipment that often arrive through Syria.
edges.context: Hezbollah depends on IRGC for funding and equipment.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, Hezbollah fought against Israeli forces and the South Lebanon Army (SLA), eventually leading to Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon in 2000.
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the SLA during the 1980s and 1990s.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah was a long time ally of the Syrian Ba'athist regime, led by the Al-Assad family until its downfall in 2024.
edges.context: Hezbollah was a long-time ally of the Syrian Ba'athist regime.

edges.evidence_quote: Hezbollah also fought against the Islamic State.
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the Islamic State in Syria.

edges.evidence_quote: On 16–17 February 2013, Syrian opposition groups claimed that Hezbollah, backed by the Syrian military, attacked three neighbouring Sunni villages controlled by the Free Syrian Army (FSA).
edges.context: Hezbollah attacked villages controlled by the Free Syrian Army.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2010, Ahbash and Hezbollah members were involved in a street battle which was perceived to be over parking issues, both groups later met to form a joint compensation fund for the victims of the conflict.
edges.context: Hezbollah and Ahbash fought in a street battle but later cooperated on a compensation fund.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2010, Ahbash and Hezbollah members were involved in a street battle which was perceived to be over parking issues, both groups later met to form a joint compensation fund for the victims of the conflict.
edges.context: Hezbollah and Ahbash later cooperated to form a joint compensation fund.

edges.evidence_quote: In the year preceding the attacks of 7 October 2023, Hezbollah, alongside Hamas and PIJ, raised funds via cryptocurrencies.
edges.context: Hezbollah raised funds via cryptocurrencies alongside Hamas.

edges.evidence_quote: PIJ and Hezbollah alone are thought to have exchanged up to US$12 million in cryptocurrency since 2021.
edges.context: Hezbollah and PIJ exchanged cryptocurrency.

edges.evidence_quote: US and Israeli counter-terrorism officials claim that Hezbollah has (or had) links to Al Qaeda, although Hezbollah's leaders deny these allegations.
edges.context: US and Israeli officials allege links between Hezbollah and Al-Qaeda, but Hezbollah denies.

edges.evidence_quote: Although Hezbollah and Hamas are not organisationally linked, Hezbollah provides military training as well as financial and moral support to the Sunni Palestinian group.
edges.context: Hezbollah provides military training, financial and moral support to Hamas.

edges.evidence_quote: On 18 January 2015, a group of Hezbollah fighters was targeted in Quneitra, with the Al-Nusra Front claiming responsibility.
edges.context: Al-Nusra Front claimed responsibility for an attack that killed Hezbollah fighters.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, Hezbollah fought against Israeli forces and the South Lebanon Army (SLA), eventually leading to Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon in 2000.
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the South Lebanon Army during the 1980s and 1990s.

edges.evidence_quote: The group was initially supported by 1,500 Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) instructors, who helped unify various Lebanese Shia factions under Hezbollah's leadership.
edges.context: IRGC provided instructors and support to unify factions under Hezbollah.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, Hezbollah fought against Israeli forces and the South Lebanon Army (SLA), eventually leading to Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon in 2000.
edges.context: Hezbollah fought against the SLA during the Israeli occupation.
